narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 83% chance that you would not pass, based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the school maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your planned part-time enrollment status, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction—unlike the vast majority of students who passed (over 93%), who attended full-time and benefited from more focused study time without work demands. Next in influence was your third-year grade decile of 3, well below the average of about 6 for those who passed, signaling to the model a notable dip in performance that raised concerns about building the rigorous skills needed for the bar. Your middle family income bracket also nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, as passers were far more commonly from upper-middle or high-income homes (over half), which the model associates with additional resources for exam prep. Your LSAT score of 33 and undergraduate GPA of 3.20 were close to averages for passers but couldn't offset these top concerns, while your first-year decile of 5 was somewhat typical. Compared to accepted students, who generally showed stronger year-three rankings, full-time commitment, and family backgrounds with more financial cushions, your profile aligned more closely with those who struggled on the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's the model highlighting risks based on historical trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above 37, the passer average, or strengthening your recent academic deciles through targeted coursework or tutoring. Switching to a full-time track could dramatically shift your odds, as it did for so many others. You might also explore building a more robust support network, like study groups or prep resources, to mimic the advantages seen in successful students from varied backgrounds. With these adjustments, reapplying could yield a very different outcome—many students in similar spots have turned things around. Keep pushing; your undergraduate record shows you have a solid foundation to build on.
